metadata.title: AI / ML Supply Chain Security Landscape
metadata.description: Comprehensive mapping of AI/ML supply chain security tools, frameworks, and standards across the full model lifecycle — from training data to production monitoring.
metadata.unifiedModel: Maps NIST AI RMF, OWASP ML/LLM Top 10, MITRE ATLAS, and emerging AI governance standards into 8 AI lifecycle phases.

nist.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology

phases.name: Training Data Sourcing & Curation
phases.description: Sourcing, curating, validating, and documenting training datasets. Ensuring data quality, detecting poisoning, managing provenance, and handling PII/copyright compliance.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: MAP 1.5, MAP 2.3 — Data provenance and quality assessment
phases.owaspMapping: ML04 — Data Poisoning, ML01 — Input Manipulation
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0020 — Poison Training Data, AML.T0019 — Publish Poisoned Dataset
threats: PII / sensitive data in training sets
Copyright / license violations in scraped data
controls: Bias auditing and fairness testing of datasets

tools.name: Croissant (MLCommons)
tools.description: Metadata format for ML datasets — describes dataset structure, provenance, licensing, and responsible AI properties in a machine-readable format.

tools.name: Data Provenance Initiative
tools.description: Large-scale audit of training datasets used in AI — maps provenance, licensing, and composition of popular datasets.

tools.name: DVC (Data Version Control)
tools.description: Git-based version control for datasets and ML experiments — tracks data lineage, enables reproducible pipelines, and manages large files.

tools.name: Great Expectations
tools.description: Data validation framework — defines expectations for data quality, schema conformance, and statistical properties with automated testing.

tools.name: Presidio (Microsoft)
tools.description: SDK for data protection and PII anonymization — detects and de-identifies PII in text and images for safe training data preparation.

tools.name: Cleanlab
tools.description: Data-centric AI tool for finding and fixing label errors, outliers, and data quality issues in ML datasets automatically.
controlsCovered: Bias auditing and fairness testing of datasets

tools.name: AI Fairness 360 (AIF360)
tools.description: IBM toolkit for detecting and mitigating bias in datasets and ML models — provides 70+ fairness metrics and 10+ bias mitigation algorithms.
controlsCovered: Bias auditing and fairness testing of datasets

tools.name: Know Your Data (Google)
tools.description: Tool for exploring and understanding ML datasets — surfaces potential biases, sensitive content, and data quality issues before training.
controlsCovered: Bias auditing and fairness testing of datasets

gaps.title: Automated data poisoning detection
gaps.description: No production-ready tool reliably detects sophisticated data poisoning or backdoor injection in large-scale training datasets.

gaps.title: Training data copyright verification
gaps.description: No automated system can determine copyright status of web-scraped or crowd-sourced training data at scale. Legal frameworks are still evolving.

gaps.title: Cross-dataset provenance standard
gaps.description: Croissant is emerging but adoption is early. No universal standard links a model's performance to specific training data subsets.

phases.name: Model Development & Training
phases.description: Securing the model training pipeline — reproducible experiments, isolated compute environments, tamper-resistant training processes, and training artifact integrity.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: MAP 3.4 — Secure development practices for AI
phases.owaspMapping: ML06 — AI Supply Chain Attacks (training phase)
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0018 — Backdoor ML Model, AML.T0035 — Develop Capabilities
threats: Malicious code in training scripts or notebooks
controls: Training environment isolation and access control

tools.name: MLflow
tools.description: Open source platform for ML lifecycle management — experiment tracking, reproducible runs, model packaging, and deployment. LF AI & Data project.

tools.name: Kubeflow
tools.description: Kubernetes-native ML workflow platform — provides pipeline orchestration, experiment tracking, and scalable training with infrastructure isolation.
controlsCovered: Training environment isolation and access control

tools.name: Determined AI
tools.description: Deep learning training platform with built-in experiment tracking, distributed training, hyperparameter search, and reproducibility features.

tools.name: DVC (pipeline mode)
tools.description: ML pipeline versioning and reproducibility — tracks code, data, parameters, and metrics together for fully reproducible training runs.

tools.name: SLSA for ML (proposal)
tools.description: Proposed extension of SLSA framework to ML training pipelines — applying build provenance concepts to model training processes.

tools.name: Metaflow
tools.description: Human-centric ML infrastructure framework (Netflix) — manages data, compute, and versioning for reproducible ML workflows.
controlsCovered: Training environment isolation and access control

gaps.title: Training pipeline tamper detection
gaps.description: No equivalent of SLSA provenance exists for ML training. Cannot cryptographically verify that a model was trained with specific code, data, and parameters.

gaps.title: Fine-tuning chain of custody
gaps.description: When models are fine-tuned across organizations, no standard tracks the full lineage from base model through each fine-tuning step.

gaps.title: GPU/TPU compute integrity
gaps.description: No mechanism to verify that training hardware has not been compromised. Side-channel attacks on GPU clusters are an emerging concern.

phases.name: Model Packaging & Signing
phases.description: Securely packaging model artifacts, signing model weights, using safe serialization formats, and generating provenance attestations for AI/ML models.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: GOVERN 1.7 — AI artifact integrity and traceability
phases.owaspMapping: ML06 — AI Supply Chain Attacks (packaging phase)
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0010 — ML Supply Chain Compromise, AML.T0017 — Develop Adversarial ML Artifacts
threats: Unsigned model weights distributed without verification
Malicious code embedded in model files
Lack of provenance for fine-tuned or merged models
Model weight extraction and unauthorized redistribution
controls: Model file format validation and scanning
Keyless signing with OIDC identity binding

tools.name: Model Signing (Sigstore)
tools.description: Applies Sigstore-based signing to ML models — generates cryptographic signatures and provenance attestations for model artifacts.
controlsCovered: Keyless signing with OIDC identity binding

tools.name: Safetensors (Hugging Face)
tools.description: Safe and fast file format for storing tensors — eliminates arbitrary code execution risks from pickle-based formats (PyTorch, TensorFlow).
controlsCovered: Model file format validation and scanning

tools.name: ModelScan (Protect AI)
tools.description: Scans ML model files for unsafe operations — detects malicious code in pickle, H5, SavedModel, and other serialization formats.
controlsCovered: Model file format validation and scanning

tools.name: ONNX Runtime
tools.description: Cross-platform inference accelerator with safe model format (ONNX protobuf) — avoids unsafe deserialization while enabling model portability.

tools.name: Cosign (for model OCI artifacts)
tools.description: Extended to sign OCI-packaged model artifacts — enables keyless signing and verification of models stored in container registries.
controlsCovered: Keyless signing with OIDC identity binding

tools.name: fickling
tools.description: Python pickle decompiler and static analyzer — detects malicious code hidden in serialized Python objects used in ML model files.
controlsCovered: Model file format validation and scanning

gaps.title: Model signing adoption
gaps.description: Sigstore Model Signing is experimental. No model hub (Hugging Face, PyTorch Hub) requires signed models by default. Adoption is near zero.

gaps.title: Fine-tuned model provenance
gaps.description: No standard for attesting the chain of custody through fine-tuning: base model → dataset → parameters → resulting model.

gaps.title: Safe format migration
gaps.description: Despite safetensors availability, many popular models still distribute in pickle format. Migration tooling exists but ecosystem-wide adoption is slow.

phases.name: Model Registry & Distribution
phases.description: Hosting, sharing, and distributing AI/ML models through registries. Ensuring access controls, integrity verification, namespace protection, and secure model delivery.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: GOVERN 1.6 — Mechanisms for secure AI artifact distribution
phases.owaspMapping: ML06 — AI Supply Chain Attacks (distribution phase)
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0010.002 — ML Supply Chain Compromise: Model Repository
threats: Malicious model uploads to public hubs
controls: Model registry access controls and MFA

tools.name: Hugging Face Hub
tools.description: Largest open model hub — hosts 500K+ models with versioning, access controls, model cards, gated models, and malware scanning.
controlsCovered: Model registry access controls and MFA

tools.name: MLflow Model Registry
tools.description: Centralized model store for managing model lifecycle — versioning, staging, annotations, and deployment tracking.

tools.name: ORAS (OCI Registry As Storage)
tools.description: Enables storing any artifact (including ML models) in OCI-compliant registries — leverages container registry security infrastructure for model distribution.

tools.name: Harbor (for ML artifacts)
tools.description: Enterprise container registry extended to host OCI-packaged ML models with vulnerability scanning, content trust, and replication.
controlsCovered: Model registry access controls and MFA

tools.name: ModelDB
tools.description: Open source model management system — tracks model versions, experiments, and metadata for reproducibility and governance.

gaps.title: Model hub security standards
gaps.description: No industry standard for model hub security requirements. Hugging Face, PyTorch Hub, and others have ad-hoc security with no common baseline.

gaps.title: Model typosquatting prevention
gaps.description: No tooling exists to detect and prevent model name squatting or typosquatting on public model hubs. Attackers can publish malicious models with similar names.

gaps.title: Cross-hub model verification
gaps.description: Models are re-hosted across multiple hubs with no standard for verifying that a copy matches the original or tracking its distribution chain.

phases.name: Model Evaluation & Red Teaming
phases.description: Adversarial testing, safety evaluation, bias auditing, and red teaming of AI/ML models before deployment. Assessing robustness, fairness, and security properties.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: MEASURE 2.6, 2.7 — AI system testing and evaluation, adversarial testing
phases.owaspMapping: LLM01 — Prompt Injection, LLM02 — Insecure Output Handling, LLM09 — Overreliance
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0043 — Craft Adversarial Data, AML.T0015 — Evade ML Model, AML.T0040 — ML Model Inference API Access
threats: Bias and fairness failures in production

tools.name: PyRIT (Microsoft)
tools.description: Python Risk Identification Toolkit — automated red teaming framework for generative AI, testing prompt injection, jailbreaks, and harmful content generation.

tools.name: Garak
tools.description: LLM vulnerability scanner — probes language models for hallucination, data leakage, prompt injection, toxicity, and other failure modes.

tools.name: Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART)
tools.description: IBM library for ML security — provides attacks, defenses, and robustness metrics for evasion, poisoning, extraction, and inference threats.

tools.name: HELM (Stanford CRFM)
tools.description: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models — comprehensive benchmark suite measuring accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, and efficiency.

tools.name: Inspect AI (UK AISI)
tools.description: Framework for large language model evaluations by UK AI Safety Institute — supports custom evaluations with built-in safety benchmarks.

tools.name: DeepEval
tools.description: Open source LLM evaluation framework — unit testing for LLMs with metrics for hallucination, toxicity, bias, answer relevancy, and faithfulness.

tools.name: MLCommons AI Safety Benchmarks
tools.description: Standardized safety evaluation benchmarks for AI systems — consortium-led effort to create widely adopted safety testing standards.

tools.name: Counterfit (Microsoft)
tools.description: Command-line tool for assessing ML model security — automates adversarial attacks against ML models using ART and TextAttack backends.

gaps.title: Standardized AI safety benchmarks
gaps.description: Multiple competing benchmark suites exist but no universal standard for 'AI safety certification.' MLCommons is working on this but adoption is early.

gaps.title: Multimodal model evaluation
gaps.description: Most red teaming tools focus on text LLMs. Evaluation of multimodal models (vision, audio, video) for adversarial robustness is severely underserved.

gaps.title: Automated red teaming maturity
gaps.description: PyRIT and Garak are promising but early. Automated red teaming cannot yet match the creativity and coverage of human red teams for novel attack vectors.

phases.name: AI BOM & Transparency
phases.description: Generating and managing AI Bills of Materials — model cards, data cards, ML-BOMs — to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and regulatory compliance.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: GOVERN 4.1 — Organizational AI transparency practices, MAP 5.1
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0010 — ML Supply Chain Compromise (transparency gap)
threats: Opaque model lineage and fine-tuning history
Missing license and usage restriction information
Lack of intended use and limitation documentation
controls: AI/ML BOM creation (CycloneDX ML-BOM or SPDX AI)

tools.name: CycloneDX ML-BOM
tools.description: Extension of CycloneDX SBOM standard for ML — captures model architecture, datasets, training environment, hyperparameters, and performance metrics.
controlsCovered: AI/ML BOM creation (CycloneDX ML-BOM or SPDX AI)

tools.name: SPDX AI Profile
tools.description: SPDX extension for AI/ML transparency — adds fields for model type, training data, metrics, known limitations, and safety information.
controlsCovered: AI/ML BOM creation (CycloneDX ML-BOM or SPDX AI)

tools.name: Model Cards (Google)
tools.description: Framework for documenting ML model performance, limitations, intended use, and ethical considerations — originated at Google Research.

tools.name: Hugging Face Model Cards
tools.description: Standardized model documentation on Hugging Face Hub — includes metadata YAML, model description, training details, and evaluation results.

tools.name: Datasheets for Datasets
tools.description: Documentation framework for datasets (Gebru et al.) — standardizes how dataset creators communicate composition, collection, and intended uses.

tools.name: CoSAI Model Card Specification
tools.description: Coalition for Secure AI working on standardized security-focused model cards — includes adversarial testing results, known vulnerabilities, and supply chain metadata.
controlsCovered: AI/ML BOM creation (CycloneDX ML-BOM or SPDX AI)

tools.name: CodeCarbon
tools.description: Tracks carbon emissions from computing — measures energy consumption and CO2 impact of model training for environmental impact reporting.

gaps.title: ML-BOM tooling
gaps.description: CycloneDX ML-BOM and SPDX AI Profile exist in draft but automated tooling for generating ML-BOMs from training pipelines is virtually non-existent.

gaps.title: Model card enforcement
gaps.description: Model cards are voluntary documentation. No model hub or registry enforces minimum documentation standards or validates model card completeness.

gaps.title: Training data transparency
gaps.description: Most foundation model providers do not disclose full training data composition. No regulatory mechanism reliably compels transparency.

phases.name: Deployment & Runtime Security
phases.description: Securing AI model serving infrastructure, protecting against prompt injection, implementing guardrails, input/output filtering, and inference pipeline hardening.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: MANAGE 2.2 — Mechanisms for monitoring and responding to AI risks in deployment
phases.owaspMapping: LLM01 — Prompt Injection, LLM02 — Insecure Output, LLM05 — Supply Chain Vulnerabilities, LLM06 — Sensitive Information Disclosure
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0051 — LLM Prompt Injection, AML.T0054 — LLM Jailbreak, AML.T0040 — ML Model Inference API Access
threats: Model theft via repeated API queries
controls: Output filtering and content safety checks
Guardrails for content safety and topic control

tools.name: NeMo Guardrails (NVIDIA)
tools.description: Programmable guardrails for LLM applications — controls topics, prevents jailbreaks, blocks harmful outputs, and enables custom safety policies.
controlsCovered: Guardrails for content safety and topic control
Output filtering and content safety checks

tools.name: LLM Guard (Protect AI)
tools.description: Self-hosted toolkit for sanitizing LLM interactions — scans prompts and outputs for prompt injection, PII, toxicity, and banned content.
controlsCovered: Output filtering and content safety checks

tools.name: Guardrails AI
tools.description: Framework for adding structural, type, and quality guarantees to LLM outputs — validates responses against schemas and safety policies.
controlsCovered: Output filtering and content safety checks
Guardrails for content safety and topic control

tools.name: Rebuff
tools.description: Self-hardening prompt injection detector — uses multi-layered defense including heuristics, LLM-based detection, and canary token injection.

tools.name: OWASP LLM Top 10
tools.description: Definitive guide to the top 10 security risks for LLM applications — provides descriptions, examples, prevention strategies, and attack scenarios.
controlsCovered: Output filtering and content safety checks
Guardrails for content safety and topic control

tools.name: LangKit (WhyLabs)
tools.description: Open source text metrics toolkit for LLM monitoring — detects prompt injection, PII, toxicity, relevance, and sentiment in real-time.
controlsCovered: Output filtering and content safety checks

tools.name: Vigil
tools.description: LLM prompt injection detection tool — uses multiple detection methods including vector similarity, YARA rules, and ML classifiers.

gaps.title: Prompt injection defense maturity
gaps.description: No defense reliably prevents all forms of prompt injection. Current tools reduce risk but determined attackers can still bypass guardrails.

gaps.title: Standardized guardrail specification
gaps.description: Each guardrail framework has its own configuration format. No standard for defining, sharing, or composing safety policies across tools.

gaps.title: Agent/tool-use security
gaps.description: LLM agents with tool access (code execution, API calls, file system) create new attack surfaces. No comprehensive framework secures agentic AI systems.

phases.name: Monitoring, Governance & Response
phases.description: Continuous monitoring of deployed AI systems, drift detection, abuse monitoring, regulatory compliance, incident response for AI-specific threats, and governance frameworks.
phases.nistAiRmfMapping: MANAGE 3.1, 3.2, 4.1 — Continuous monitoring, incident response, compliance
phases.owaspMapping: ML09 — Output Integrity Attack, ML08 — Model Skewing
phases.mitreAtlasMapping: AML.T0031 — Erode ML Model Integrity, AML.T0024 — Exfiltration via ML Inference API
threats: Model drift degrading accuracy over time
Adversarial abuse at scale in production
Lack of incident response playbooks for AI
controls: Abuse pattern detection and rate limiting

tools.name: Evidently AI
tools.description: Open source ML observability platform — monitors data drift, model performance, target drift, and data quality with interactive dashboards.

tools.name: WhyLabs / whylogs
tools.description: Data and ML monitoring library — profiles datasets and model outputs for drift detection, data quality monitoring, and anomaly detection.

tools.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
tools.description: Comprehensive AI risk management framework — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE functions for organizations deploying AI systems responsibly.

tools.name: MITRE ATLAS
tools.description: Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems — knowledge base of adversarial techniques, tactics, and case studies specific to ML systems.
controlsCovered: Abuse pattern detection and rate limiting

tools.name: CoSAI (Coalition for Secure AI)
tools.description: OASIS collaboration developing comprehensive AI security frameworks — includes governance, risk assessment, and incident response standards.

tools.name: AI Incident Database
tools.description: Public database tracking AI incidents and controversies — enables pattern analysis and lessons learned for AI incident response.

tools.name: ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System)
tools.description: International standard for AI management systems — provides requirements for establishing, implementing, and improving AI governance within organizations.

tools.name: OWASP ML Top 10
tools.description: Top 10 security risks for machine learning systems — covers input manipulation, data poisoning, model stealing, and supply chain attacks.
controlsCovered: Abuse pattern detection and rate limiting

gaps.title: AI-specific incident response
gaps.description: No standardized incident response framework exists for AI-specific incidents (model poisoning, adversarial attacks, prompt injection at scale). Traditional IR doesn't apply.

gaps.title: EU AI Act compliance tooling
gaps.description: EU AI Act is in effect but automated compliance checking and evidence generation tools are immature. Organizations must manually assess conformity.

gaps.title: Cross-org AI incident coordination
gaps.description: No equivalent of CERT or CSAF for AI-specific incidents. When a foundation model has a security issue, no standard process coordinates response across downstream users.

gaps.title: Real-time adversarial detection at scale
gaps.description: Detecting adversarial inputs in real-time production traffic without unacceptable latency overhead remains an unsolved engineering challenge.

crossCuttingGaps.title: End-to-End AI Supply Chain Standard
crossCuttingGaps.description: No equivalent of SLSA exists for AI. No framework provides graduated levels of supply chain security assurance across the full ML lifecycle from data to deployment.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Foundation Model Transparency
crossCuttingGaps.description: Most foundation model providers do not disclose training data, training process details, or comprehensive safety evaluation results. Downstream users cannot assess supply chain risk.

crossCuttingGaps.title: AI Security Tooling Fragmentation
crossCuttingGaps.description: Dozens of overlapping tools with different interfaces, configuration formats, and coverage gaps. No unified security pipeline spans evaluation through production monitoring.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Fine-Tuning Chain of Custody
crossCuttingGaps.description: Models are fine-tuned, merged, quantized, and distilled across organizations. No standard tracks the full provenance chain from base model through each transformation.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Agentic AI Security
crossCuttingGaps.description: LLM agents with tool access (code execution, web browsing, API calls) create new supply chain attack surfaces that existing frameworks do not address.

crossCuttingGaps.title: Regulatory Alignment
crossCuttingGaps.description: EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and other regulations have different requirements. No unified compliance framework maps across all regulatory regimes.

frameworks.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
frameworks.description: Comprehensive voluntary framework for managing AI risks through GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, and MANAGE functions. Aligned with US Executive Order on AI.

frameworks.name: MITRE ATLAS (Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems)
frameworks.description: Knowledge base of adversarial ML tactics and techniques — AI-specific extension of ATT&CK framework for understanding and defending against AI threats.

frameworks.name: OWASP ML Top 10
frameworks.description: Top 10 security risks for ML systems — input manipulation, data poisoning, model inversion, membership inference, model theft, and supply chain attacks.

frameworks.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications
frameworks.description: Top 10 risks specific to LLM-powered applications — prompt injection, insecure output handling, training data poisoning, model DoS, and supply chain vulnerabilities.

frameworks.name: EU AI Act
frameworks.description: European Union regulation on AI — risk-based classification system requiring conformity assessments, transparency obligations, and documentation for high-risk AI systems.

frameworks.name: ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management System)
frameworks.description: International standard for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an AI management system within an organization.

frameworks.name: Coalition for Secure AI (CoSAI)
frameworks.description: OASIS-hosted initiative bringing together industry leaders to develop practical AI security specifications and tools.
